content.text: Azure offers a broad portfolio of managed database services spanning relational SQL engines, NoSQL stores, in-memory caches, and analytics platforms. All of these are fully managed PaaS offerings: Azure handles hardware provisioning, OS patching, backup, high availability, and software version upgrades. You configure, connect, and query; Azure operates the infrastructure.

attrs.alt: Azure database services in three groups: Relational SQL (Azure SQL Database, SQL Managed Instance, PostgreSQL, MySQL), NoSQL/Distributed (Cosmos DB multi-API, Redis Cache), and Analytics (Synapse Analytics, Databricks, Azure Data Explorer)

content.text: Azure SQL Database is the fully managed PaaS version of SQL Server. It runs the SQL Server Database Engine, supports T-SQL, and provides features like Always On availability, automatic backups, transparent data encryption, and intelligent query processing. Azure manages the OS, SQL Server instance, patching, and HA failover — you provision a database and connect to it.

Three purchasing models are available: DTU (Database Transaction Units — a blended measure of CPU, memory, and I/O bundled into tiers), vCore (explicit vCPU count with choices of Provisioned or Serverless compute), and Hyperscale (vCore model with distributed storage that scales to 100 TB and decouples compute from storage). For most new workloads, choose vCore Provisioned or Serverless.
One database with its own compute and storage
Multiple databases sharing a pool of DTUs or vCores
Multi-tenant SaaS with variable load across tenants
Full SQL Server instance in a VNet (not SQL Database PaaS)
Lift-and-shift of on-prem SQL Server with linked servers, CLR, SQL Agent
Compute scales to zero when idle; billed per second of use
Intermittently used databases with unpredictable workloads
Up to 100 TB, named replicas, fast restore
Very large OLTP databases, reporting with read replicas

# Create an Azure SQL Database (vCore, General Purpose, 2 vCores, serverless)
az sql server create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mysqlserver \
  --location eastus \
  --admin-user sqladmin \
  --admin-password "SecureP@ssword1!"

az sql db create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mysqlserver \
  --name mydb \
  --edition GeneralPurpose \
  --family Gen5 \
  --capacity 2 \
  --compute-model Serverless \
  --auto-pause-delay 60 \
  --min-capacity 0.5

# Allow your client IP to connect through the server firewall
az sql server firewall-rule create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mysqlserver \
  --name AllowMyIP \
  --start-ip-address 203.0.113.5 \
  --end-ip-address 203.0.113.5

# List all databases on a server
az sql db list \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mysqlserver \
  --query "[].{name:name, tier:sku.tier, vCores:sku.capacity, state:status}" \
  --output table

# Scale a database to more vCores
az sql db update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mysqlserver \
  --name mydb \
  --capacity 4

# Create a geo-replica in a secondary region
az sql db replica create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mysqlserver \
  --name mydb \
  --partner-server mysqlserver-secondary \
  --partner-resource-group myRG-secondary \
  --service-objective GeneralPurpose

# Initiate failover to the geo-secondary (planned failover — no data loss)
az sql db replica set-primary \
  --resource-group myRG-secondary \
  --server mysqlserver-secondary \
  --name mydb
Azure Database for PostgreSQL and MySQL

Azure Database for PostgreSQL - Flexible Server and Azure Database for MySQL - Flexible Server are managed open-source database services. Both provide the community database engine (no proprietary extensions or vendor lock-in), with Azure managing the infrastructure. Flexible Server supports zone-redundant high availability (standby in a different Availability Zone), configurable maintenance windows, and up to 64 TB of storage.

# Create a PostgreSQL Flexible Server (zone-redundant HA)
az postgres flexible-server create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mypostgres \
  --location eastus \
  --admin-user pgadmin \
  --admin-password "SecureP@ss1!" \
  --sku-name Standard_D4s_v3 \
  --tier GeneralPurpose \
  --storage-size 128 \
  --version 15 \
  --high-availability ZoneRedundant \
  --standby-zone 2

# Create a database on the server
az postgres flexible-server db create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server-name mypostgres \
  --database-name appdb

# Show server details including FQDN (for connection string)
az postgres flexible-server show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mypostgres \
  --query "{fqdn:fullyQualifiedDomainName, version:version, sku:sku.name, ha:highAvailability.mode}" \
  --output json

# Create a read replica in the same region
az postgres flexible-server replica create \
  --replica-name mypostgres-replica \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --source-server mypostgres

# Create a MySQL Flexible Server (HA, version 8.0)
az mysql flexible-server create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mymysql \
  --location eastus \
  --admin-user mysqladmin \
  --admin-password "SecureP@ss1!" \
  --sku-name Standard_D4s_v3 \
  --tier GeneralPurpose \
  --storage-size 128 \
  --version 8.0.21 \
  --high-availability ZoneRedundant \
  --standby-zone 2

Azure Cosmos DB is Microsoft's globally distributed, multi-model NoSQL database service. It provides single-digit millisecond reads at the 99th percentile at any scale, with turnkey global distribution across all Azure regions — you add regions with a click and Cosmos DB replicates data automatically. Consistency is configurable on a five-level spectrum: from Strong (linearizable, highest latency) to Eventual (highest availability, lowest latency), with three intermediate levels (Bounded Staleness, Session, Consistent Prefix).

Cosmos DB supports five APIs that let you use familiar client libraries and wire protocols: Core (SQL) API for document JSON with SQL-like queries; MongoDB API for applications using the MongoDB driver; Cassandra API for Cassandra CQL clients; Gremlin API for graph databases; and Table API as a Cosmos-backed replacement for Azure Table Storage.
Reads always return the latest committed write; single-region effective
Reads lag by at most K versions or T seconds behind writes
Real-time leaderboards, inventory with bounded lag
Within a session, reads honor your own writes
Most web and mobile apps — the practical default
No ordering guarantees; reads may return stale data

# Create a Cosmos DB account (Core SQL API, two regions, Session consistency)
az cosmosdb create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mycosmosaccount \
  --kind GlobalDocumentDB \
  --locations regionName=eastus failoverPriority=0 isZoneRedundant=true \
  --locations regionName=westeurope failoverPriority=1 isZoneRedundant=false \
  --default-consistency-level Session \
  --enable-automatic-failover true

# Create a database and container (with partition key and provisioned RU/s)
az cosmosdb sql database create \
  --account-name mycosmosaccount \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mydb

az cosmosdb sql container create \
  --account-name mycosmosaccount \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --database-name mydb \
  --name orders \
  --partition-key-path "/customerId" \
  --throughput 1000

# Enable autoscale on an existing container (RU/s scales 100–10,000 automatically)
az cosmosdb sql container throughput update \
  --account-name mycosmosaccount \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --database-name mydb \
  --name orders \
  --max-throughput 10000

# Add a third region
az cosmosdb update \
  --name mycosmosaccount \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --locations regionName=eastus failoverPriority=0 isZoneRedundant=true \
  --locations regionName=westeurope failoverPriority=1 isZoneRedundant=false \
  --locations regionName=southeastasia failoverPriority=2 isZoneRedundant=false

# View current throughput and RU consumption metrics
az cosmosdb sql container throughput show \
  --account-name mycosmosaccount \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --database-name mydb \
  --name orders \
  --query "{current:resource.throughput, autoscale:resource.autoscaleSettings.maxThroughput}" \
  --output json

Azure Cache for Redis is a fully managed in-memory data store based on the Redis open-source project. It provides sub-millisecond data access for caching database query results, session state, output from expensive computations, and pub/sub messaging. Azure manages cluster provisioning, OS patching, backup, and replication — you configure the cache tier, connect with a Redis client, and use standard Redis commands.

# Create a Standard tier Redis cache (2-node HA, 1 GB)
az redis create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myredis \
  --location eastus \
  --sku Standard \
  --vm-size c1 \
  --redis-version 7

# Get connection details (hostname and primary access key)
az redis show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myredis \
  --query "{host:hostName, port:sslPort, tlsVersion:minimumTlsVersion}" \
  --output json

az redis list-keys \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myredis \
  --query primaryKey \
  --output tsv

# Enable persistence on a Premium cache (saves RDB snapshots to linked Storage Account)
az redis update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mypremiumredis \
  --set redisConfiguration.rdb-backup-enabled=true \
  redisConfiguration.rdb-backup-frequency=60 \
  redisConfiguration.rdb-storage-connection-string="<storage_conn_str>"

# Flush all keys (useful for testing; destructive in production)
az redis force-reboot \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myredis \
  --reboot-type AllNodes
New .NET/Java app using SQL Server features (T-SQL, stored procedures)
Azure SQL Database (vCore Serverless or Provisioned)
Lift-and-shift of existing SQL Server with linked servers, SQL Agent, CLR
Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server
Azure Database for MySQL Flexible Server
Global, multi-region writes at any scale, JSON documents
Existing MongoDB application migrating to Azure
Graph relationships (social networks, fraud detection, knowledge graphs)
Sub-millisecond cache for session state, query results, real-time data
Petabyte-scale analytics, data lakehouse, SQL + Spark in one platform
Optimized Apache Spark, Delta Lake, MLflow
Time-series, telemetry, log analytics at scale with KQL

All Azure managed database services provide encryption at rest (using Azure Storage Service Encryption with Microsoft-managed or customer-managed keys) and encryption in transit (TLS 1.2 minimum). In addition, several services provide advanced security features for enterprise and compliance workloads.

detects anomalous activities indicating unusual and potentially harmful attempts to access or exploit databases. Alerts on SQL injection attacks, brute force login attempts, and access from an unexpected principal. Available for Azure SQL Database, SQL Managed Instance, and SQL Server on Azure VMs.
tracks database and server events to a Log Analytics workspace, Azure Storage, or Event Hub. Required by PCI-DSS, HIPAA, and SOC 2 for evidence of access to sensitive data.

a client-side encryption feature that encrypts specific columns in SQL Server before the data leaves the application — even the database engine and DBA cannot read the plaintext. Implemented in the JDBC/ODBC/ADO.NET drivers.

T-SQL predicate functions that filter rows from query results based on the executing user's identity. No application code changes required — the filter applies transparently at the database engine level.
masks sensitive columns (credit card numbers, email addresses, SSNs) in query results for low-privilege users without changing the stored data. Masking rules are applied at the database engine layer.
encrypts the entire database file on disk. Enabled by default on all new Azure SQL Database and SQL Managed Instance deployments.

# Enable Microsoft Defender for SQL on a server
az security atp storage update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --storage-account mystorageaccount \
  --is-enabled true

# Enable SQL Auditing to a Log Analytics workspace
az sql server audit-policy update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mysqlserver \
  --state Enabled \
  --log-analytics-target-state Enabled \
  --log-analytics-workspace-resource-id $(az monitor log-analytics workspace show -g myRG -n myLAW --query id -o tsv)

# Enable database-level auditing (overrides server policy for this database)
az sql db audit-policy update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mysqlserver \
  --name mydb \
  --state Enabled \
  --storage-account mystorageaccount \
  --retention-days 90

# Enable TDE with a customer-managed key (CMK) from Key Vault
az sql server tde-key set \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mysqlserver \
  --server-key-type AzureKeyVault \
  --kid "https://mykeyvault.vault.azure.net/keys/myTDEKey/version"

# Enable Advanced Threat Protection on a PostgreSQL Flexible Server
az postgres flexible-server threat-protection-setting update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mypostgres \
  --state Enabled

# Add a Cosmos DB VNet rule to restrict access to a specific subnet
SUBNET_ID=$(az network vnet subnet show -g myRG --vnet-name myVNet -n AppSubnet --query id -o tsv)
az cosmosdb network-rule add \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mycosmosaccount \
  --subnet "$SUBNET_ID" \
  --virtual-network myVNet

Azure managed database services provide built-in HA at the service level — you configure the tier, and Azure handles the failover. Understanding the HA mechanism for each service helps you set appropriate RPO (Recovery Point Objective) and RTO (Recovery Time Objective) targets.
Always On Availability Group across 3 replicas in 3 AZs
Always On AG within instance cluster

# Create an Azure SQL Database failover group (automatic geo-failover)
# 1. Create secondary server in another region
az sql server create \
  --resource-group myRG-secondary \
  --name mysqlserver-secondary \
  --location westeurope \
  --admin-user sqladmin \
  --admin-password "SecureP@ssword1!"

# 2. Create a failover group (auto-failover after 60 minutes of primary unavailability)
az sql failover-group create \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mysqlserver \
  --name myfailovergroup \
  --partner-server mysqlserver-secondary \
  --failover-policy Automatic \
  --grace-period 60 \
  --add-db mydb

# 3. The secondary database listener endpoint is: myfailovergroup.secondary.database.windows.net

# View failover group status
az sql failover-group show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mysqlserver \
  --name myfailovergroup \
  --query "{role:replicationRole, partnerServers:partnerServers[0].replicationRole}" \
  --output json

# Manually force failover to secondary (for planned maintenance)
az sql failover-group set-primary \
  --resource-group myRG-secondary \
  --server mysqlserver-secondary \
  --name myfailovergroup

# Create a Cosmos DB account with automatic failover across 3 regions
az cosmosdb update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mycosmosaccount \
  --enable-automatic-failover true \
  --locations regionName=eastus failoverPriority=0 isZoneRedundant=true \
  --locations regionName=westeurope failoverPriority=1 isZoneRedundant=false \
  --locations regionName=southeastasia failoverPriority=2 isZoneRedundant=false

# Enable zone-redundant HA for PostgreSQL Flexible Server (must be done at creation)
# To upgrade an existing non-HA server to ZoneRedundant HA:
az postgres flexible-server update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mypostgres \
  --high-availability ZoneRedundant \
  --standby-zone 2

Azure managed database services perform automated backups that you can use to restore to any point within the retention window without any prior setup — no backup agents, no backup scripts, no tapes. The backup and restore capabilities differ by service.

full backups weekly, differential backups every 12–24 hours, and transaction log backups every 5–12 minutes. Retention window: 7 days (configurable to 35 days). Point-in-time restore (PITR) creates a new database from the backup at the target timestamp.
full weekly backups plus incremental backups. Default retention 7 days (configurable to 35 days). PITR restores to a new server.
same backup cadence as SQL Database. PITR restores to a new managed instance or the same instance.
continuous backup mode enables PITR within the last 30 days at 1-second granularity. Periodic backup mode (legacy) takes snapshots every 4 hours and retains for 8 hours.
RDB snapshots can be taken every 15, 30, 60, 360, 720, or 1440 minutes and stored in an Azure Storage Account. AOF (Append-Only File) provides more durable persistence but impacts latency.

# Change backup retention on an Azure SQL Database (up to 35 days)
az sql db str-policy set \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mysqlserver \
  --name mydb \
  --retention-days 28

# Configure long-term backup retention (LTR) — keep a weekly backup for 2 years
az sql db ltr-policy set \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mysqlserver \
  --name mydb \
  --weekly-retention P8W \
  --monthly-retention P6M \
  --yearly-retention P2Y \
  --week-of-year 1

# List available LTR backups
az sql db ltr-backup list \
  --location eastus \
  --server mysqlserver \
  --database mydb \
  --output table

# Restore a SQL Database to a point in time (creates a new database)
az sql db restore \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --server mysqlserver \
  --name mydb-restored \
  --source-database mydb \
  --time "2026-06-29T14:00:00Z"

# Restore PostgreSQL Flexible Server to a point in time
az postgres flexible-server restore \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mypostgres-restored \
  --source-server mypostgres \
  --restore-time "2026-06-29T14:00:00Z"

# Enable Cosmos DB continuous backup (PITR within 30 days, 1-second granularity)
az cosmosdb update \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name mycosmosaccount \
  --backup-policy-type Continuous \
  --continuous-tier Continuous30Days

# Restore a Cosmos DB account to a point in time
az cosmosdb restore \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --target-database-account-name mycosmosaccount-restored \
  --account-name mycosmosaccount \
  --restore-timestamp "2026-06-29T14:00:00Z" \
  --location eastus

Azure's database portfolio covers every major workload category: relational SQL, open-source engines, globally distributed NoSQL, in-memory cache, and big data analytics. All services are fully managed PaaS — Azure handles infrastructure, backups, patching, and high availability.

Azure SQL Database is the go-to for SQL Server workloads in the cloud; Serverless compute scales to zero for intermittent usage. SQL Managed Instance provides the highest SQL Server compatibility for lift-and-shift.
Azure Database for PostgreSQL and MySQL Flexible Servers provide managed open-source engines with zone-redundant HA and read replicas.
Azure Cosmos DB is the choice for globally distributed, multi-model NoSQL data; the five consistency levels let you trade latency for correctness based on your workload's requirements.
Azure Cache for Redis provides sub-millisecond in-memory access for caching, session state, and pub/sub; tiers scale from Basic (dev) to Enterprise (active-active multi-region).
Azure Synapse Analytics and Azure Databricks serve large-scale analytics and data engineering on data lakes.
